nativeRange.habitat: Small, slow-moving forest streams, coastal swamps, marshes, and shallow water holes with dense surface vegetation and low flow

diet.notes: Micropredator that tends to prefer small live and frozen foods. Has been documented accepting baby brine shrimp, daphnia, copepods, micro-worms, and crushed micro-pellets or flake. Freeze-dried daphnia and frozen cyclops are commonly accepted. Live or frozen micro-foods are generally recommended for best condition and color.

summary: Tiny West African killifish with bold torpedo-striped body and fan-shaped, brilliantly colored tail. A peaceful nano-tank surface dweller best kept in soft, acidic water with floating plants and gentle filtration.
careNotes:
Non-annual killifish: unlike Nothobranchius and other annual killifish, Epiplatys annulatus does not undergo a dry-season diapause and tends to live 2-5 years in well-maintained aquariums. The species is native to small forest streams and swamps in Sierra Leone, Guinea, and Liberia where water is soft, acidic, and very slow-moving.

Taxonomic note: formerly placed in the monotypic genus Pseudepiplatys (as Pseudepiplatys annulatus). Current taxonomy (FishBase, Catalog of Fishes) places it back in Epiplatys. Older literature and some retailers may still use Pseudepiplatys annulatus.

Water chemistry: this species generally does best in soft, acidic water (pH 4.0-6.5 is commonly preferred in the hobby; the upper tolerance extends to ~7.0 but harder or more alkaline conditions tend to reduce vitality and color over time). Soft water (gH 1-8) and low kH (0-4) are generally advised. The tank should use gentle filtration — a sponge filter or small air-driven filter is commonly recommended.

Escape risk note: Aquarium Co-Op recommends a tight-fitting lid for this species. However, unlike most killifish, the Clown Killifish has short, fan-shaped fins that limit jumping ability, and it is generally considered a lower escape risk than typical killifish. A secure lid is still advisable as a precaution and to prevent evaporation losses.

Behavior: males have been known to display toward each other with fin-spreading but rarely inflict damage. A trio (1M:2F) or small group works well in a 30-40L planted nano tank. Good tankmates include other nano species of similar size — micro-rasboras, small tetras, pygmy corydoras, and dwarf shrimp (adults only; fry may be predated). NOT recommended with boisterous or aggressive species.

pH sources disagreed: FishBase reports 6.5-7.0; Fishkeeping World reports 4.0-7.0. User-provided guidance indicates preferred range is 4.0-6.5. The composite range 4.0-7.0 is used for the parameter field to reflect the full tolerance; the preferred range is noted above in prose.

Lifespan sources disagreed: Aquarium Co-Op states 2-3 years; Fishkeeping World states approximately 5 years. Range of 2-5 years recorded to reflect the spread.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported as moderate in difficulty. This species tends to scatter eggs among fine-leaved or floating plants; Java moss, hornwort, or spawning mops are commonly used. Eggs generally hatch in approximately 10-14 days at typical tank temperatures. Fry are tiny and have been reported to require infusoria or commercial first-foods such as vinegar eels and paramecium before graduating to newly hatched brine shrimp. Separating adults from eggs or raising fry in a dedicated container is generally advised as adults will consume eggs and fry. A small group can produce spawns regularly under good conditions.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy, native range, adult size (4.0 cm max), temperature (23-25°C), pH (6.5-7.0), and hardness (dH ≤5). SeriouslyFish (first listed secondary) returned HTTP 520 and was not usable.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Third secondary used after SeriouslyFish (HTTP 520) and Practical Fishkeeping (HTTP 403). Confirmed native range (Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone), adult size (3-3.5 cm), temperature (20-26°C), pH (4-7), and compatibility notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Plant-spawner; fry require infusoria or micro-foods initially. See breedingNotes for full details.
